items.title: Trump signs executive order reclassifying about 8,000 federal positions
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order placing roughly 8,000 senior federal positions into a new at-will category called Schedule Policy/Career.
items.details: On June 3, 2026, the order reclassified about 8,000 senior policy-influencing positions into Schedule Policy/Career, removing certain civil service protections. The administration described it as an accountability measure; federal unions and employee organizations criticized it. OPM gave agencies seven days to make conforming changes to personnel records.

items.title: White House issues executive order on advanced AI innovation and security
items.summary: The White House issued an executive order on June 2, 2026, addressing artificial intelligence innovation and national security considerations.
items.details: The order, titled "Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security," directs agencies to strengthen cyber defenses and develop within 60 days a voluntary framework for government review of frontier AI models. Under the framework, developers would provide the government access to leading-edge models 30 days before wider release.

items.title: Judge declines to block administration's $1.8 billion settlement fund
items.summary: A federal judge rejected a watchdog group's request to temporarily block a new federal settlement fund for people claiming to be victims of a weaponized government.
items.details: On June 10, 2026, a federal judge declined to issue a temporary order blocking the Trump administration from proceeding with a roughly $1.776 billion fund tied to a settlement of the president's lawsuit against the IRS. The fund had drawn opposition from some members of Congress, including some Republicans.

items.title: U.S. and Iran exchange new strikes as ceasefire frays
items.summary: The United States and Iran traded strikes for a third time in a week, with President Trump threatening further military action.
items.details: On June 10–11, 2026, the U.S. launched strikes on multiple targets in Iran after a reported downing of a U.S. helicopter, and Iran said it launched drones and missiles at U.S.-linked sites in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan. Iran's Foreign Ministry said the U.S. attacks had rendered the ceasefire meaningless.

items.title: U.S. military enforces blockade of Iranian oil shipments
items.summary: U.S. Central Command said it fired missiles to disable a tanker attempting to transport Iranian oil, as the U.S. maintained a blockade of Iranian ports.
items.details: On June 11, 2026, the U.S. military said it struck a Guinea-Bissau-flagged tanker after its crew failed to obey orders. Trump said the U.S. had been conducting a mission to move oil shipments past Iran's forces in the Strait of Hormuz and threatened to assume control of Iran's oil and gas sectors, including Kharg Island.

items.title: World Bank cuts global growth forecast citing Iran war
items.summary: The World Bank lowered its forecast for global economic growth, citing fallout from the Iran war and disruptions to oil shipping.
items.details: Disruption of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has affected global oil markets and trade. Reporting on June 11, 2026, noted the World Bank cut its global growth forecast, while European and U.S. markets reacted to the conflict's economic effects.

items.title: Trump signs Secure America Act funding immigration enforcement
items.summary: President Trump signed the Secure America Act, providing about $70 billion for ICE and Customs and Border Protection through fiscal year 2029.
items.details: Signed June 10, 2026, the reconciliation measure allocates roughly $38.5 billion to ICE and about $22–26 billion to CBP, with additional contingency funds. The Senate passed it 52-47 on June 5 and the House 214-212 on June 9, largely along party lines, with Sen. Lisa Murkowski voting against it.

items.title: House passes TRUE Accountability Act unanimously
items.summary: The House passed the TRUE Accountability Act under suspension of the rules during a week focused on anti-fraud bills.
items.details: On June 8, 2026, the House passed H.R. 8466, the TRUE Accountability Act, by a vote of 384-0 under a procedure used for less controversial bills. It was among several measures the House considered that week aimed at addressing various types of fraud.

items.title: Secret Service and ballroom funding dropped from reconciliation bill
items.summary: Proposed additions to the Secure America Act, including funds tied to a White House ballroom project, were not included in the final measure.
items.details: A proposed $1 billion for the Secret Service tied to White House ballroom security and a separate $1.8 billion fund were dropped before passage. The Senate parliamentarian ruled the additional Secret Service funding violated the Byrd Rule, which restricts extraneous provisions in reconciliation bills.

items.title: Supreme Court allows Alabama to use congressional map for 2026
items.summary: The Supreme Court cleared the way for Alabama to use a congressional map that a lower court had found racially discriminatory.
items.details: On June 2, 2026, in an unsigned order, the Court overturned a three-judge panel and allowed Alabama to use a map producing six Republican-leaning districts and one Democratic-leaning district. The Court's three liberal justices dissented, with Justice Sotomayor writing separately.

items.title: Supreme Court issues decisions in several cases
items.summary: The Supreme Court released opinions in multiple cases on June 4, 2026, including matters involving the FCC, the SEC, and pharmaceutical patents.
items.details: Decisions issued June 4, 2026, included FCC v. AT&T, in which the Court held the FCC may issue forfeiture orders without a jury under the Seventh Amendment, and Sripetch v. SEC regarding disgorgement remedies. The Court has multiple major cases remaining before its summer recess.

items.title: Judge to review settlement of Trump's lawsuit against the IRS
items.summary: A federal judge in Florida said she would review a Justice Department settlement of President Trump's lawsuit against the IRS.
items.details: U.S. District Judge Kathleen Williams ordered the president's lawyers to respond by June 12 to a motion filed by 35 retired federal judges challenging the agreement. A separate judge in Virginia temporarily blocked the related fund's establishment at least until June 12.

items.title: Four states hold primaries shaping 2026 midterms
items.summary: Maine, South Carolina, Nevada and North Dakota held primary elections on June 9, 2026.
items.details: In Maine, Graham Platner won the Democratic U.S. Senate primary, setting up a November contest with Republican Sen. Susan Collins. In South Carolina, Rep. Nancy Mace did not advance in the Republican gubernatorial primary, which heads to a June 23 runoff between Pamela Evette and Alan Wilson.

items.title: South Carolina governor calls special session on redistricting
items.summary: South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster called a special legislative session to address redrawing the state's congressional map.
items.details: Following the Supreme Court's April ruling narrowing the Voting Rights Act, McMaster issued an executive order convening lawmakers, citing unfinished debate on redistricting. Republicans were expected to consider a map that could shift the state's sole Democratic-leaning district. Some GOP state senators opposed a redraw.

items.title: Supreme Court order spurs Southern redistricting efforts
items.summary: Following the Court's Alabama order and earlier Voting Rights Act ruling, several states moved to revise congressional maps ahead of 2026.
items.details: The June 2, 2026 Alabama order allowed use of a map removing a majority-Black district. Alabama Gov. Kay Ivey rescheduled primaries in the 2nd, 6th and 7th Congressional Districts. Multiple states have enacted or considered new maps amid mid-decade redistricting disputes.

AL.title: Alabama to use reinstated congressional map after primaries rescheduled
AL.summary: Alabama rescheduled primaries in three congressional districts after the Supreme Court allowed use of a legislatively drawn map.
AL.details: Gov. Kay Ivey rescheduled primaries in the state's 2nd, 6th and 7th Congressional Districts after the Supreme Court's June 2, 2026 order halted a judicial ruling that had required two majority-Black districts. The primaries had been scheduled for May 19.

CA.title: Newsom signs election protection law SB 73
CA.summary: California Gov. Gavin Newsom signed Senate Bill 73, intended to strengthen the state's election safeguards.
CA.details: Signed May 27, 2026, ahead of the June 2 statewide primary, the law adds protections against election interference and intimidation and addresses access to voting systems, voter rolls, and polling places. Supporters described it as a response to federal interference concerns.
CA.source: Office of the Governor of California

CT.title: Lamont signs youth online safety and AI law
CT.summary: Connecticut Gov. Ned Lamont signed Public Act 26-15 addressing youth online safety, AI regulation, and workforce training.
CT.details: Signed June 2, 2026, the bipartisan law establishes protections related to youth social media use and artificial intelligence and creates workforce training initiatives. It was developed in collaboration with the attorney general and a state senator who co-chairs the General Law committee.

IA.title: Reynolds signs final bills of 2026 session
IA.summary: Iowa Gov. Kim Reynolds signed the final bills of the 2026 legislative session, the last of her tenure.
IA.details: On June 2, 2026, Reynolds signed dozens of bills, including measures on immigration-related citizenship checks and gubernatorial powers, and vetoed at least one. She is not seeking another term in the 2026 election.

ME.title: Platner wins Maine Democratic Senate primary
ME.summary: Graham Platner won Maine's Democratic U.S. Senate primary on June 9, 2026.
ME.details: Platner, an oyster farmer and military veteran, won the nomination to face Republican Sen. Susan Collins in November in a race seen as significant to Senate control. The contest drew national attention amid reporting on past conduct allegations.

SC.title: Mace falls short in South Carolina governor primary
SC.summary: Rep. Nancy Mace did not advance in South Carolina's Republican gubernatorial primary, which heads to a runoff.
SC.details: On June 9, 2026, Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette and state Attorney General Alan Wilson advanced to a June 23 Republican runoff. The winner will face Democratic state Rep. Jermaine Johnson. Gov. Henry McMaster separately called a special session on congressional redistricting.

MS.title: Mississippi forms committees to study redistricting
MS.summary: Mississippi legislative leaders formed committees to study redistricting after a planned special session was canceled.
MS.details: After the Supreme Court's Voting Rights Act ruling, Gov. Tate Reeves canceled a special session on the state Supreme Court map. Lt. Gov. Delbert Hosemann created a Senate Select Committee on Redistricting, and House Speaker Jason White announced six committees to study the issue ahead of the 2027 session.

LA.title: Landry signs early career exposure bill
LA.summary: Louisiana Gov. Jeff Landry signed House Bill 286 requiring early career exposure activities in K-12 schools.
LA.details: Signed in late May 2026, the law received unanimous bipartisan support in both legislative chambers. It requires activities such as career interest surveys and classroom visits from career professionals for students in kindergarten through 12th grade.

FL.title: Florida's new congressional map in effect for 2026
FL.summary: Florida's redrawn congressional map, signed in May, is set to be used in the 2026 election amid ongoing litigation.
FL.details: Gov. Ron DeSantis signed the map (HB 1-D) on May 4, 2026, after the Legislature approved a 24–4 plan targeting four incumbent Democrats. A lawsuit challenging the map remains pending while it is in use for the 2026 cycle.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited sources and does not endorse any party, official, or position.
